records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 143 
Chapter 8 
Special Studies: Q&A; Women in the Pañjī; Dooshan 
Pañjī and the Political Economy of Status 
The Panji text outlines thePanjiPrabandha, an extensive genealogical 
system established in the 14th century to document the lineage 
ofMaithilBrahminsand other communities in the Mithila region. Initiated 
under the reign ofKingHarisimhadeva, this record-keeping tradition was 
designed to prevent prohibited marriages by tracking ancestral details 
across multiple generations. The sources detail a complex structure 
ofgotras(clans) andmools(ancestral villages), categorising families into 
specific social and hierarchical tiers based on their heritage. Essential to 
this system is theUtedh, a genealogical chart used by specialized 
registrars known asPanjikarsto verify marital eligibility through a 
rigorous examination of maternal and paternal lines. Beyond marriage 
laws, the text reflects the preservation ofMaithila cultureand the Tirhuta 
script, illustrating a sophisticated historical effort to maintain 
community identity and genetic purity. Modern efforts to digitise and 
transliterate these ancient palm-leaf manuscripts aim to protect 
this1,500-year-old genealogical heritage from physical decay and linguistic 
obsolescence. 
How did the Panji Prabandha system reorganise Maithil society in 1326? 
In 1326 AD (Shaka 1248), the ruler of Mithila, Maharaja Harisimhadeva, 
alongside contemporary scholars, introduced thePanjiPrabandha system to 
radically reorganize Maithil society and its marital practices. This 
reorganization was triggered by the decline of the older family-maintained 
genealogical record system (Samuha Lekhya), which had led to people 
unknowingly entering into unscriptural marriages within forbidden degrees 
of kinship. 
The Panji Prabandha system reorganized Maithil society in several key ways: 
• 
Centralization of Genealogical Records: The responsibility of 
maintaining family lineages was taken away from individual families and 
institutionalized as an official state department.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 144 
• 
Creation of the 'Panjikar' Class: The king appointed specialized scholars 
known as Panjikars (genealogists) who traveled to each family to collect 
detailed ancestral information. The comprehensive written records 
containing data on every new birth and marriage became known as the 
Panji, and the citizens registered within these records were designated as 
Panjibaddha. 
• 
Mandatory Marriage Verification: It became mandatory to obtain an 
authorization certificate (Siddhanta Patra or Aswajana Patra) from a 
state-appointed Panjikar before finalizing any marriage. This ensured the 
bride and groom did not fall within forbidden degrees of consanguinity—
specifically, avoiding five generations on the mother's side and six to 
seven generations on the father's side. Any marriage without this 
certificate was considered unscriptural. 
• 
Social Stratification and Hierarchy: While Harisimhadeva's original 
system was intended to be a purely scientific and egalitarian genealogical 
record across Brahmins, Kayasthas, and Kshatriyas, it fundamentally 
reorganized Maithil Brahmins into distinct hierarchical categories. 
Society was divided into classifications such as Shrotriya (the highest 
tier), Yogya, and Panjibaddha (or Vamshaj). 
• 
Long-Term Societal Shifts: Over the centuries, this rigid classification 
system led to deep internal stratification and a decline in the democratic 
values of Mithila. It eventually gave rise to the Bikaua class (high-
ranking men who married multiple times to confer their social status on 
lower-ranking families) and contributed to severe societal issues like 
child marriage and a significantly high number of widows. 
Explain the 'Utedh' rules for determining marriage eligibility between 
families. 
The'Utedh'(or Utteh) rules form the fundamental genealogical framework 
used by Maithil Panjikars (genealogists) to determine strict marriage 
eligibility between a prospective bride and groom. Before a marriage can be 
formally authorised with a Siddhanta (authorisation certificate), the Panjikar 
must construct an 'Utedh Panji' for both families to meticulously map their 
lineages and ensure they do not violate scriptural rules of consanguinity. 
The rules and structure of the Utedh calculation process involve the 
following key components:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 145 
1. The 32Moolsand Ancestral MappingTo determine eligibility, the Utedh 
maps out32 ancestral*Mools* (root families) for both the bride and the 
groom. The chart introduces seven generations of ancestors, 
systematically detailing the lineages of the paternal and maternal 
grandfathers, grandmothers, and their respective ancestors. Specifically, 
the Utedh tracessix generations on the paternal sideandfive generations on 
the maternal side for both parties to map out their complete biological 
lineage. 
2. Formatting theUtedhChartTo create the Utedh, the Panjikar writes the 
bride's village and Mool village on the left side of the chart. Directly next to 
this, the specific Mool is listed, followed by the names of her ancestors in 
descending chronological order—starting from the great-great-great-
grandfather down to the father. A structurally identical Utedh chart is 
simultaneously created for the groom. 
3. The 16ChhathiCalculationThe most critical rule of the Utedh is 
calculating the16Chhathi. The Chhathi represents the 16 specific ancestors 
(drawn from both the maternal and paternal lines) from whom the bride or 
groom is exactly the sixth descendant. 
• 
The Rule of Exclusion: Once the bride's 16 Chhathi are identified, they 
are cross-referenced with the groom's Utedh. If even one of the bride's 16 
Chhathi ancestors appears within the groom's paternal lineage (Pitru-
Paksha) within six generations, the marriage is strictly forbidden. 
• 
The Exception: If the overlapping ancestor appears exclusively on the 
groom's maternal side (Matru-Paksha), the shared ancestry (Sapinda) is 
considered ceased, and the marriage is permitted. 
4. Additional ProhibitionsAlongside the Chhathi rules, the Utedh enforces 
three additional strict prohibitions to authorise a marriage: 
• 
The bride and groom must not belong to the same Gotra (patrilineal 
clan). 
• 
The bride and groom must not share the same Pravara (sage lineage 
associated with their Gotra). 
• 
The bride must not be the offspring of the groom's step-mother's brother 
(a rule locally known as avoiding the Kathmama's lineage). 
By cross-referencing these Utedh charts, the Panjikars scientifically ensure 
that the bloodlines are sufficiently distanced and all religious and genetic 
boundaries are respected before allowing the families to unite.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 146 
What are the roles of Gotras, Pravaras and Muls in lineage? 
Gotrasdefine the broad patrilineal ancestry within Maithil Brahmin society. 
Every Gotra is named after the earliest known great sage (Mahapurush) of 
that lineage, and belonging to the same Gotra indicates a shared patrilineal 
bloodline. There are 20 Gotras in total, with seven being particularly 
prominent and well-established, such as Shandilya, Vatsa, Kashyap, Savarna, 
Parashar, Bharadwaj, and Katyayan. In the context of lineage and marriage, 
sharing the same Gotra strictly prohibits a marital union, ensuring genetic 
distance between families. 
Pravarasrepresent the specific revered ancient sages from a family's lineage 
who are recognized as the composers of Rigvedic hymns. Maithil Brahmins 
are divided into two main Pravara categories based on their Gotra: those with 
three ancestral sages (Tri-pravara) and those with five (Panch-pravara). 
Historically, Pravaras were recited during Yagnas (fire rituals) to invoke the 
sacred fire. Much like Gotras, Pravaras act as a strict boundary for marital 
eligibility; a bride and groom must not share the same Pravara to be 
authorized for marriage. 
Muls(Mools) pinpoint the exact geographical origin or the most ancient 
known village where the founding ancestor (Biji Purush) of a specific family 
branch originally settled. While Gotras identify the broad sage lineage, Muls 
identify the specific, localized sub-branch of that family. Historical records 
identify 167 distinct Muls scattered across various villages, which later 
evolved into a hierarchy where 34 or 35 were considered primary Muls for 
higher-ranking families. 
The Mul is the most critical component for maintaining genealogical records 
(Panji). For instance, tracing a person's complete biological lineage to create 
an Utedh chart requires mapping out 32 ancestral Muls (tracing back 
grandparents and great-grandparents on both the maternal and paternal sides) 
to verify marriage eligibility. Furthermore, as families migrated, different 
branches of the same family were often identified by compounding their 
original Mul village with their newer settled village to prevent losing their 
exact lineage. While a Mul generally aligns with one specific Gotra, there are 
exceptions; for example, the Brahmpura Mul contains families belonging to 
multiple different Gotras (Shandilya, Vatsa, Kashyap, Alambukaksh, 
Gautam, and Garg). 
Who are the thirty-two ancestral figures mentioned in the records?

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 147 
The thirty-two ancestral figures mentioned in the records refer to the thirty-
two root families (Kulas or Mools) that are meticulously mapped out to 
construct the Utedh chart, which details seven generations of a person's 
biological lineage 
. 
To verify marital eligibility and maintain the purity of the bloodline, the 
genealogists trace the ancestry back to these specific thirty-two figures, 
which include: 
The paternal grandfather and paternal grandmother of the person's father and 
mother 
. 
The paternal grandfather and paternal grandmother of the person's maternal 
grandfather and maternal grandmother 
. 
The fathers of the person's mother and maternal grandmother 
. 
The primary purpose of mapping these specific ancestors is to scientifically 
guarantee genetic distance. According to the rigid rules of the Panji 
Prabandha, for a marriage to be authorized, the prospective bride or groom 
must fall exactly in the seventh generation (seventh position) descending 
from these thirty-two ancestors 
. Mapping these thirty-two root branches ensures that the families are 
sufficiently distanced and do not violate the forbidden degrees of 
consanguinity. 
What are the specific rules regarding mother's and father's lineages? 
The specific rules regarding the mother's and father's lineages dictate the 
acceptable genetic distance (Sapindata) required to authorize a marriage 
under the Panji Prabandh system. Based on ancient Dharmashastra texts like 
the 
Manu 
Smriti, 
the 
primary 
directive 
governing 
this 
is 
*"MatrutahPanchamiTyaktva,PitrutahSaptami"* (abandon the fifth from 
the mother, abandon the seventh from the father). 
The rules are strictly defined as follows: 
• 
Maternal Lineage (Matru-paksha): A prospective bride and groom must 
not share an ancestor within five generations on the mother's side. Any

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 148 
overlapping ancestor within this five-generation boundary prohibits the 
marriage. The shared ancestry is considered to cease after the fifth 
generation, making the sixth generation scientifically eligible for a 
marital union. 
• 
Paternal Lineage (Pitru-paksha): The bloodline on the father's side is 
traced further back. A bride and groom must not share an ancestor within 
seven generations on the paternal side. While the original scriptural rule 
strictly excludes up to the seventh generation, some later records and 
regional modifications (such as those by Maharaja Mahesh Thakur) 
adjusted this to excluding six generations and accepting the seventh 
generation for marriage. 
In practice, this means a bride cannot be a descendant within five generations 
of any man from the groom's maternal clan, nor can she be a descendant 
within seven (or six, as amended) generations of the groom's paternal clan. If 
these conditions are met alongside the Gotra and Pravara rules, the families 
are considered sufficiently distanced to marry. 
How did Maharaja Harisimhadeva select the original Panjikars? 
Maharaja Harisimhadeva selected the original Panjikars through ajoint 
decision with the contemporary learned scholarsof his kingdom. After 
convening an assembly to address the societal crisis of unscriptural 
marriages, the scholars and the king concluded that the vital task of 
managing genealogical records should be entrusted only to themost capable 
and 
qualified 
individuals 
in 
society 
who 
were 
experts 
inDharmashastra(religious texts) and deeply committed to protecting 
Dharma. 
Following these criteria, the king established an official state department and 
appointed the first Panjikars byroyal decree (Rajagnya). The original 
selections were specifically divided by caste: 
• 
For the Maithil Brahmins: He appointed Sudhyaya Gunakar Jha of the 
Mahindrapur Pandua Mool. The king specifically sought out Gunakar 
Jha because he had already been voluntarily collecting and maintaining 
genealogical records of the Brahmin community on his own initiative. 
• 
For the Karna Kayasthas: He appointed Shankardatt. 
• 
For the Kshatriyas: He appointed Vijaydatt.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 149 
These selected experts were officially designated as Panjikars and were 
granted the exclusive state authority to verify bloodlines and issue the 
necessary authorization certificates before any marriage could take place. 
What is the significance of the 16 Chhathi ancestors? 
The 16 Chhathi refer to the 16 specific ancestors (drawn from both the 
maternal and paternal lineages) from whom a prospective bride or groom is 
exactly the sixth descendant 
. They are the most critical component in calculating marriage eligibility 
within the Panji Prabandha system. 
The significance of the 16 Chhathi lies in the following strict rules: 
Foundation of Marital Verification: Before a marriage can be authorised, a 
Panjikar (genealogist) must first meticulously calculate and identify the 
bride's 16 Chhathi 
. Once identified, these ancestors are cross-referenced with the groom's 
Utedh (genealogical chart) to ensure the bloodlines do not overlap within 
forbidden degrees 
. 
The Rule of Exclusion: If even one of the bride's 16 Chhathi ancestors 
appears anywhere within the groom's paternal lineage (Pitru-paksha) within 
six generations, the marriage is strictly prohibited 
. 
The Maternal Exception: If the overlapping Chhathi ancestor appears 
exclusively within the groom's maternal lineage (Matru-paksha), the shared 
ancestry is considered to have ceased, and the marital union is permitted 
. 
Equal Weight and Mutual Application: All 16 Chhathi hold equal 
importance, and no single ancestor among them is given special privilege or 
exemption under scriptural rules 
. Furthermore, this strict genealogical cross-referencing applies equally to 
both the bride and the groom to guarantee scientific genetic distance between 
the two families. 
How does the system define the same 'Gotra' prohibition? 
In the Panji Prabandha system, the prohibition against marrying within the 
same Gotra is rooted in the concept of shared patrilineal ancestry.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 150 
The system defines the prohibition through the following principles: 
• 
Lineage and Blood Relation: A Gotra acts as a marker of a family's 
patriarchal lineage (Vamsha-bodhak), indicating that all its members are 
descendants of a single, earliest known great sage (Mahapurush or 
Muni). Therefore, belonging to the same Gotra implies a direct blood 
relationship (Rakta Sambandha) among its members. For example, 
anyone belonging to the Shandilya Gotra is considered a direct 
descendant of the sage Shandilya. 
• 
The Rule of Prohibition: To prevent marriages between closely related 
bloodlines, the system strictly dictates that a prospective bride and groom 
must not belong to the same Gotra (Saman Gotra). 
• 
Scriptural Basis: This explicit prohibition serves as one of the primary 
rules for determining marriage eligibility and is heavily based on the 
directives of ancient Dharmashastra texts and scholars like Manu and 
Yajnavalkya. 
Before any marriage can be authorized, the genealogists (Panjikars) must 
verify that the bride and groom do not share the same Gotra, ensuring that the 
necessary genetic and religious boundaries are respected. 
What are the three tiers of Maithil Brahmin hierarchy? 
The Maithil Brahmin society was hierarchically divided into three primary 
tiers based on their genealogical registration (Panji): 
• 
Shrotriya: This is considered the highest tier within the hierarchy. 
• 
Yogya: This constitutes the second or middle tier. 
• 
Panjibaddha (also known as Vamshaj): This is the third tier, comprising 
the remaining registered families. 
Additionally, the historical records note that individuals who werenot 
registered within the Panji system were classified separately as 
'Jaewar'(or Jaebar). Despite these strict hierarchical divisions, the 
fundamental Dharmashastra rules regarding marriage and religious conduct 
applied equally across the Shrotriya, Yogya, Panjibaddha, and Jaewar 
categories. 
Over time, this hierarchy developed further internal stratifications; for 
example, theShrotriyatier was sub-divided into 8 distinct categories, while 
theYogya andVamshajtiers were divided into 15 categories. 
How did the 1326 reorganisation create the Shrotriya hierarchy?

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 151 
The 1326 reorganisation under Maharaja Harisimhadeva did not actually 
create the Shrotriya hierarchy. When the Panji Prabandha system was first 
established in 1326, it was a purely scientific and egalitarian genealogical 
record, and all Brahmins, Kayasthas, and Kshatriyas were treated as equals 
within their respective caste records. During this early period, only the Mool 
(root) books were used, and their sole purpose was to determine scriptural 
marital eligibility. 
The hierarchical division into categories like Shrotriya, along with the rigid 
ranking of "high" and "low" families, emerged centuries later during the 
reign of Maharaja Madhav Singh. Around 1760 AD, certain intellectuals 
influenced the king to order the Panjikars (genealogists) to compile the 
*ShakhaPustaks* (branch books). 
It was the introduction of this Shakha Panji system that officially partitioned 
the society and created these hierarchical classifications, which unfortunately 
gave rise to various social malpractices. As a direct result of these later 
modifications, theShrotriyaofficially emerged as a distinct new Brahmin 
sub-caste in Mithila around 1800 AD, long after the original 1326 
reorganisation. 
Explain the role of Biji Purush in genealogical records. 
In the Maithil genealogical records (Panji Prabandha), aBijiPurush(bījī 
puruṣa) refers to theearliest known founding ancestor of a specific family 
lineage. The identification of the Biji Purush serves as the fundamental 
anchor for mapping a family's genetic history. 
His role in the records is defined by the following key aspects: 
• 
Establishing the Mool (Root Village): The concept of the Biji Purush is 
intrinsically tied to the Mool Gram (root village). The specific, ancient 
village where this founding ancestor originally resided and settled 
became the permanent Mool for all of his future descendants. 
• 
Unifying Scattered Family Branches: Over centuries, as families grew, 
members naturally migrated and settled in various new villages, creating 
different family branches (Shakhas). However, the Panji records trace all 
these scattered individuals back to their original Mool Gram to prove 
they all descend from the same Biji Purush. For instance, historical 
records proved that two distinct branches residing in the villages of 
Mangarauni and Garh actually belonged to the same Mandar Mool, 
meaning they shared the exact same Biji Purush.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 152 
• 
Scientific Tracking of Lineage: By identifying the Biji Purush, 
genealogists (Panjikars) can compile a complete, unbroken biological 
history of a family's roots. This precise historical tracking is what allows 
them to confidently map out the 32 ancestral roots (Utedh) required to 
verify marriage eligibility and prevent unions within forbidden degrees 
of blood relation. 
The historical records meticulously list the specific Biji Purush for various 
Mools to keep the lineages distinct. For example: 
• 
The Biji Purush of the Gangoli Mool is Gangadhar. 
• 
The Biji Purush of the Khandwala Mool is Sankarshan. 
• 
The Biji Purush of the Mandar Mool is Trinayan Bhatt. 
While a family's Gotra indicates their broad descent from an ancient revered 
sage, tracing the lineage back to the Biji Purush pinpoints the exact localized, 
historical patriarch who founded their specific sub-branch. 
What was Maharaja Madhav Singh's role in the shakha system? 
Maharaja Madhav Singh played the central role in establishing 
theShakhaPanji (branch books) system around 1760 AD. While the original 
Panji Prabandha introduced by Maharaja Harisimhadeva in 1326 was an 
egalitarian system that recorded all families equally in the Mul Panji, 
Maharaja Madhav Singh fundamentally altered this structure. 
Under the influence of certain "intellectually driven" or "luxurious" 
individuals at his court, Madhav Singh officially ordered the Panjikars 
(genealogists) to compile the new *ShakhaPustaks*. His role and its impact 
on the system included: 
• 
Creation of Separate Records: Prior to his reign, the Mul Panji was made 
equally for all families purely to determine marital eligibility. Madhav 
Singh mandated the creation of distinct, separate Shakha Panjis 
specifically tailored to divide families into categories such as Soti 
(Shrotriya), Jog (Yogya), and Panjibaddha. 
• 
Institutionalizing Hierarchy: His order marked the beginning of a rigid 
hierarchical division within Maithil Brahmin society. The Shakha system 
officially categorized and ranked families as "high" and "low," 
discarding the egalitarian nature of the original records. 
• 
Long-Term Social Impact: By systematically partitioning society through 
these branch books, his decree eventually led to the formal emergence of

rigid stratification gave rise to various deep-rooted social malpractices 
that subsequently plagued the community. 
How did the later reorganisations lead to social malpractices? 
The later reorganisations, specifically the introduction of the Shakha Panji 
system during Maharaja Madhav Singh's reign, fundamentally altered the 
original scientific purpose of the records by imposing rigid "high" and "low" 
hierarchical classifications. This shift led to severe internal stratification and 
a sharp decline in the democratic values of Mithila. 
This rigid hierarchy directly fueled several devastating social malpractices: 
• 
Rise of the Bikaua Class and Polygamy: The strict ranking system 
birthed the Bikaua class, where high-ranking men essentially sold their 
social status through multiple marriages. The original scientific and 
genealogical strictness of the Panji system was completely sidelined to 
accommodate this rampant malpractice of polygamy. 
• 
Absurd Relational Dynamics: Due to these excessive marriages across 
different families, men became tangled in absurd and unscriptural 
relationships. A man could simultaneously become a father-in-law or 
son-in-law to one family and a brother-in-law (sadhu) to another. The 
Panjikars (genealogists) were left helpless as these chaotic unions openly 
violated the strict scriptural rules of consanguinity they were supposed to 
enforce. 
• 
Decline in Panjikars' Integrity: The corruption of the system 
compromised the genealogists themselves, leading to a noticeable 
decline in their selfless service, truthfulness, and trustworthiness. 
• 
Child Marriage and the Widow Crisis: Driven by families' desperate need 
to secure high-ranking grooms to elevate or maintain their status, the 
toxic practice of child marriage became deeply entrenched. 
Consequently, this led to an unprecedented and tragic increase in the 
number of widows within the community. 
The historical records note that Maithil society continues to suffer from the 
long-term consequences of these malpractices, especially since strong social 
reform movements advocating for widow remarriage or opposing child 
marriage never truly materialized in the region. 
Tell me more about the Panjikar and the branch books they maintain.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 154 
ThePanjikarsare the officially designated genealogists of Mithila, and 
theShakhaPustaks(branch books) they maintain are considered an 
unprecedented genealogical record system globally, tracing complex family 
histories over a span of more than 1, years. 
The Role and Evolution of thePanjikars 
• 
Original Appointment: The first Panjikars were appointed in 1326 AD by 
Maharaja Harisimhadeva. To manage the records across different 
communities, the king appointed Gunakar Jha for the Maithil Brahmins, 
Shankardatt for the Karna Kayasthas, and Vijaydatt for the Kshatriyas. 
• 
Marriage Authorization: Their primary responsibility was to verify 
marital eligibility by mathematically ensuring that prospective brides and 
grooms did not share ancestors within forbidden degrees. If a union was 
scripturally permissible, the Panjikar would issue an authorization 
certificate known as a Siddhanta or Aswajana Patra. 
• 
Hardships and Dedication: Historically, Panjikars showed immense 
dedication. They travelled extensively from village to village to collect 
and cross-reference family data, often enduring great hardships without 
any state financial support or protection. 
• 
Decline in Integrity: Over time, particularly as hierarchical ranks became 
more important than scriptural rules, the Panjikars' integrity declined. As 
the Bikaua class (men marrying multiple times for social status) 
emerged, Panjikars found themselves helpless and were forced to 
authorize unscriptural and absurd marital relationships, compromising 
their traditional truthfulness. Some modern Panjikars are also noted to sit 
at home and arbitrarily fill out records rather than traveling to collect 
accurate data. 
TheShakhaPustaks(Branch Books)The original Panji system relied on a 
purely egalitarian Mul Panji (root book). However, around 1760 AD, 
Maharaja Madhav Singh ordered the Panjikars to compile theShakhaPustak 
(branch books) specifically to separate society into hierarchical categories 
like Soti (Shrotriya), Jog (Yogya), and Panjibaddha. 
The branch books have several unique and highly structured features: 
• 
Detailed Personal Histories: The Shakha Panji is not just a list of names. 
It provides a comprehensive (Sangopang) description of individuals, 
documenting their education, titles, special attributes, changes in 
occupation, and detailed maternal lineages.

• 
Alphabetical Indexing and Roots: The books begin with an alphabetical 
index detailing the Gotras (sage lineages), the Mools (root families), the 
Moolgrams (founding villages), and the founding ancestors (Biji Purush) 
from whom the branches originated. 
• 
Sophisticated Numbering System (Ankan): Because the lineages are vast 
and scattered, the branch books rely heavily on a complex numerical 
cross-referencing system. If a person's marriage is recorded, the book 
assigns a specific page and line number (e.g., "38/5", meaning page 38, 
line 5). If a reader needs to find the maternal or paternal ancestry of that 
person, the text provides the exact numerical coordinates to jump to an 
earlier or later page to trace the unbroken lineage. 
• 
Seven Types of Panji: The broader Panji system, which the Shakha 
Pustak is a part of, actually consists of seven distinct types of records: 
Mool (roots), Shakha (branches), Gotra, Patra, Dooshan (books recording 
flaws or "blemishes" in a lineage), Utedh (consanguinity calculations), 
and Aswajanya Patra Panji. 
Which families were assigned to the original 32 root Mools? 
To clarify a slight misconception: the "32 root Mools" mentioned in the 
genealogical records do not refer to a fixed list of 32 original families for the 
entire society. Instead, as part of the Utedh calculation, "32 Mools" refers to 
the 32 specific ancestral positions (the great-great-great-grandparents) that 
must be mapped out for each individual bride and groom to verify their 
genetic distance. 
At a societal level, the Panji records actually identify a total of167 
distinctMools (root families) among Maithil Brahmins. 
Out of these 167, historical records highlight34 or 35 primaryMools (34 
main roots plus the Khanam branch of the Fandah Mool) that were 
exceptionally prominent. These primary root families were later divided into 
a strict three-tier hierarchy:

considered the highest and included the following root families:

How do Panjikars trace maternal ancestors in the Utedh chart?

In the Utedh chart, Panjikars trace the maternal lineage (Matru-paksha) 
backfive generationsto ensure no overlapping bloodlines (Sapindata) exist 
between the prospective bride and groom. 
To map this out accurately, Panjikars mathematically calculate 16 specific 
ancestral positions (the Chhathi) from whom the bride or groom is the sixth 
descendant. While the first eight Chhathi map the father's lineage, the9th 
through the 16thChhathi specifically isolate and trace the complex branches 
of the mother's biological ancestry. 
According to the genealogical records, these maternal ancestors are traced as 
follows: 
• 
9th Chhathi: The maternal grandfather's great-grandfather (Matamaha's 
Prapitamaha). 
• 
10th Chhathi: The great-maternal grandfather's maternal grandfather 
(Pramatamaha's Matamaha). 
• 
11th Chhathi: The maternal grandfather's great-maternal grandfather 
(Matamaha's Pramatamaha). 
• 
12th Chhathi: The maternal grandfather's maternal grandmother's 
maternal grandfather (Matamaha's Matru-matamaha). 
• 
13th Chhathi: The maternal grandmother's great-grandfather (Matamahi's 
Prapitamaha). 
• 
14th Chhathi: The maternal grandmother's paternal maternal-grandfather 
(Matamahi's Pitru-matamaha). 
• 
15th Chhathi: The maternal grandmother's great-maternal grandfather 
(Matamahi's Pramatamaha). 
• 
16th Chhathi: The maternal grandmother's maternal grandmother's 
maternal grandfather (Matamahi's Matru-mahi's Matru-matamaha). 
By identifying these specific root figures on the mother's side alongside the 
paternal figures, the Panjikars mathematically guarantee the scriptural rule of 
*"MatrutahPanchamiTyaktva"* (abandoning five generations from the 
mother's side) is strictly respected before a marriage can be authorized. 
What specifically defines a sixth-generation descendant or 'Chhathi'? 
In the Panji Prabandha system, a 'Chhathi' specifically defines an ancestor 
from whom a prospective bride or groom is exactly thesixth descendant(or 
in the sixth position).

There are exactly16 such male ancestors—collectively known as the 16 
Chhathi—who are meticulously calculated by combining both the paternal 
(Pitru-kula) and maternal (Matru-kula) lineages of the individual. 
The genealogical records strictly define these 16 specific ancestral positions. 
For example, a bride's 16 Chhathi consist of the following ancestors: 
• 
Her great-grandfather's grandfather (the Vriddha Prapitamaha, who is 
considered the first Chhathi). 
• 
The maternal grandfather of her great-grandfather. 
• 
The father of her grandfather's maternal grandfather. 
• 
The father-in-law of her grandfather's maternal grandfather. 
• 
Her grandmother's great-grandfather. 
• 
The maternal grandfather of her father's maternal grandfather. 
• 
Her grandmother's great-maternal grandfather, and the maternal 
grandfather of her grandmother's maternal grandmother. 
• 
Ancestors on the mother's side, such as her maternal grandfather's great-
grandfather, up to the maternal grandfather of her maternal 
grandmother's maternal grandmother (who is the 16th Chhathi). 
To verify if a marriage is scripturally permissible, the Panjikar (genealogist) 
must identify all 16 of these Chhathi for the bride.If even one of these 16 
specific sixth-generation ancestors appears anywhere within the groom's 
paternal side (Pitru*-paksha), the marriage is strictly prohibited*. The rules 
clearly state that all 16 Chhathi hold equal authority, and no single ancestor 
among them is given any special privilege or exemption. 
What are the specific generational rules for paternal and maternal lines? 
The generational rules for paternal and maternal lines define the acceptable 
genetic distance (Sapindata) required to authorize a marriage in the Panji 
Prabandha system. These rules are deeply rooted in ancient texts like the 
Manu 
Smriti 
and 
are 
summarized 
by 
the 
primary 
directive:"*MatrutahPanchamiTyaktva,PitrutahSaptami"*(abandon the 
fifth from the mother, accept the seventh from the father). 
The specific generational boundaries are enforced as follows: 
• 
Maternal Lineage (Matru-paksha): According to the scriptures, shared 
ancestry ceases after the fifth generation (Panchamat Urdhvam). 
Therefore, a marriage is strictly prohibited if a prospective bride and 
groom share an ancestor within five generations on the mother's side.

Because the exclusion applies to five generations, the sixth-generation 
descendant (the Chhathi) on the maternal side is mathematically eligible 
for marriage. 
• 
Paternal Lineage (Pitru-paksha): The required genetic distance is greater 
on the father's side. The original scriptural rule dictates that families must 
abandon seven generations of shared ancestry on the paternal side. 
• 
The Paternal Amendment: Later in history, a prominent scholar and 
ruler, M.M. Maharaja Mahesh Thakur, introduced a regional 
modification to the paternal rule. He established that while six 
generations must be strictly excluded, the seventh generation can be 
deemed acceptable for a marital union. This modified arrangement 
became widely accepted by Maithil society. 
In practice, the genealogists ensure these boundaries are not breached by 
mapping out the 32 ancestral Mools and identifying the 16 Chhathi ancestors. 
This scientifically guarantees that a bride is not a descendant within five 
generations of any man from the groom's maternal clan, nor within seven (or 
the amended six) generations of the groom's paternal clan. 
How does the '16 Chhathi' calculation determine if a marriage is forbidden? 
The '16 Chhathi' calculation determines if a marriage is forbidden by 
meticulously cross-referencing the specific sixth-generation ancestors of the 
prospective bride and groom. 
The rules of this calculation dictate marriage eligibility as follows: 
• 
Identification of the 16 Chhathi: The genealogist (Panjikar) must first 
mathematically identify the 16 specific ancestors—combining both the 
maternal and paternal clans—from whom the bride or groom is exactly 
the sixth descendant . 
• 
The Paternal Prohibition: Once identified, the bride's 16 Chhathi are 
checked against the groom's genealogical chart. If even one of these 16 
Chhathi ancestors appears anywhere within the groom's paternal lineage 
(Pitru-paksha) within six generations, the marriage is strictly forbidden ,,. 
• 
The Maternal Exception: If the overlapping Chhathi ancestor appears 
exclusively in the groom's maternal lineage (Matru-paksha), the marriage 
is authorized ,,. 
• 
Equal Application and Weight: This strict verification rule applies 
equally to both the bride and the groom . Furthermore, all 16 Chhathi 
ancestors hold equal importance and authority; scriptural rules do not

ancestor among the sixteen ,, 
Why was Maharaja Madhav Singh's introduction of Shakha Pustaks 
significant? 
Maharaja Madhav Singh's introduction of the *ShakhaPustaks (branch 
books) around 1760 AD was significant because it permanently transformed 
the Panji Prabandha from a purely scientific and egalitarian genealogical 
record into a rigid tool for social stratification. Prior to his decree, the records 
consisted solely of the Mul Panji*, which treated all registered families as 
absolute equals and was used exclusively to verify scriptural marital 
eligibility. 
Under the influence of certain courtiers, Madhav Singh ordered the Panjikars 
(genealogists) to compile separate *ShakhaPanjis* specifically designed to 
divide society into hierarchical categories of "high" and "low," such 
asSoti(Shrotriya),Jog(Yogya), andPanjibaddha. 
This reorganization had two major areas of significance: 
1. Devastating SocialConsequences The institutionalisation of this hierarchy 
directly contradicted the original democratic values of Mithila and triggered 
severe, long-lasting social malpractices. The rigid focus on familial rank 
gave rise to the Bikaua class, where high-ranking men essentially sold their 
social status by engaging in rampant polygamy with lower-ranking families. 
Consequently, the desperate need for families to secure high-ranking grooms 
entrenched the practice of child marriage, which in turn led to an 
unprecedented and tragic increase in the widow population throughout the 
region. 
2. Unprecedented Genealogical ComplexityDespite their negative societal 
impact, the *ShakhaPustaks are recognised as a globally unprecedented 
genealogical achievement, successfully mapping interconnected family 
networks over a span of 1, years. These branch books went far beyond 
recording names; they provided comprehensive (Sangopang) personal 
histories, detailing an individual's education (Vidya), titles (Upadhi), special 
attributes, changes in occupation, and complete maternal lineages. To 
navigate this vast amount of scattered data, the ShakhaPustaks* utilized a 
highly 
sophisticated 
numerical 
cross-referencing 
system, 
allowing 
genealogists to trace an individual's unbroken biological lineage by jumping 
precisely between specific pages and lines across the records.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 161 
Who were Gunakar Jha, Shankardatt and Vijaydatt? 
Gunakar Jha,Shankardatt, andVijaydattwere the first official Panjikars 
(genealogists) appointed by Maharaja Harisimhadeva of the Karnat dynasty 
around 1326 AD to formally establish and manage the Panji Prabandha 
(genealogical record system) in Mithila. They were granted the exclusive 
state authority to maintain family lineages and scientifically verify marital 
eligibility before any marriage could be authorized. 
To effectively manage the records across the society, the king specifically 
divided their appointments by community: 
• 
Sudhyaya Gunakar Jha was appointed as the official genealogist for the 
Maithil Brahmins. Belonging to the Mahindrapur Pandua Mool, he was 
specifically sought out and chosen for this role because he had already 
been voluntarily collecting and preserving the genealogical history of the 
Brahmin community on his own initiative. 
• 
Shankardatt was appointed to maintain the genealogical records for the 
Karna Kayasthas. 
• 
Vijaydatt was appointed to maintain the genealogical records for the 
Kshatriyas. 
Together, these three experts formed the foundational institution of the 
Panjikar system, ensuring that marriages across Mithila were conducted 
strictly according to scriptural rules of consanguinity and genetic distance. 
What was the significance of Sudhyaya Gunakar Jha's prior work? 
The significance of Sudhyaya Gunakar Jha's prior work lies in the fact that it 
served as the very foundation for the institutionalised Panji Prabandha 
system. Belonging to the Mahindrapur Pandua Mool, Gunakar Jha had 
already been voluntarily engaging in the meticulous collection and 
preservation of the genealogical histories of the Brahmins and other classes 
entirely on his own initiative. 
His prior work was significant for the following reasons: 
• 
Foundation of the Official System: His voluntary, systematic collection 
of family lineages caught the attention of the Karnat dynasty ruler, 
Maharaja Harisimhadeva. When the king and his assembly of 
contemporary scholars recognized the urgent need to protect scriptural 
marriage rules from being violated, they discovered Gunakar Jha's

records.text:

Gajendra Thakur — 162 
existing body of work and officially entrusted him with the management 
of the Panji Prabandha. 
• 
The First Royal Appointment: Because of his pre-existing expertise and 
dedication to genealogical tracking, the king issued a royal decree 
(Rajagnya) appointing him as the very first official Panjikar (genealogist) 
for the Maithil Brahmins. 
• 
A Lasting Legacy: Gunakar Jha is recognised as the creator of the current 
form of the Panji Prabandha. The firm vow he took to collect, expand, 
and protect the genealogies of the Maithil society with immense 
dedication established a robust framework that has remained safely 
preserved, respected, and continuously utilized for centuries. 
How did Panjikars verify ancestral information across different castes? 
To verify ancestral information across different castes, the Panjikars utilised 
a meticulously structured, community-specific approach to data collection 
and genealogical calculation. When Maharaja Harisimhadeva established the 
Panji Prabandha in 1326 AD, the system was scientifically designed to 
encompass three main castes: the Maithil Brahmins, the Karna Kayasthas, 
and the Kshatriyas (Gandhavariya Rajputs). 
The verification process was executed through the following methods: 
1. Dedicated CastePanjikarsand Ground-Level Data CollectionTo ensure 
accurate mapping, the King appointed specific chief genealogists for each 
caste: Gunakar Jha for the Brahmins, Shankardatt for the Kayasthas, and 
Vijaydatt for the Kshatriyas. The genealogists (or Parichetas) travelled 
extensively from village to village, directly interviewing the head of each 
family. They recorded whatever ancestral memory the family possessed—
often going back up to six generations or more—and mapped this data to 
identify each family's founding ancestor (Biji Purush), root village (Mool 
Gram), Gotra, and Pravara. 
2. Caste-Specific Verification RulesWhile the fundamental goal of avoiding 
marriages within forbidden bloodlines was the same, the application differed 
slightly based on the caste: 
• 
Maithil Brahmins: As discussed earlier, Panjikars mathematically 
verified eligibility by calculating the Utedh chart based on a family's 
Gotra, Pravara, and Mool, ensuring a bride and groom did not share any 
of the 16 Chhathi ancestors within the forbidden degrees.

Gajendra Thakur — 163 
• 
Karna Kayasthas: The verification process for this community had a 
unique scientific nuance. Because all Karna Kayasthas belong to a single 
Gotra (the Kashyap Gotra), the standard prohibition against marrying 
within the same Gotra could not be applied. Therefore, for Kayasthas, the 
Panjikars calculated the Utedh and verified marital eligibility exclusively 
based on the Mool (root family lineage). 
• 
Kshatriyas: Initially, they were equally registered in the scientific Panji 
records. However, over time, the formal written Panji system became 
extinct among the Kshatriyas. Despite this, they continued to 
independently practice genealogical verification by strictly avoiding 
marriages in the specific root villages of their ancestors (such as the 
paternal grandmother's village) to prevent consanguinity. 
Initially, this process was purely egalitarian; all Brahmins, Kayasthas, and 
Kshatriyas were recorded as absolute equals within their respective caste 
registers, with the sole purpose of authorising scripturally sound marriages. 
What is the rule for the groom's step-mother's brother? 
In the Panji Prabandha system, the specific rule regarding the groom's step-
mother's 
brother 
(traditionally 
referred 
to 
as 
theKathamamorKashta*Matul*) states thata prospective bride must not 
be the offspring of the groom's step-mother's brother. 
This explicit prohibition serves as the fourth primary directive (Niyam 4) for 
determining marital eligibility. It is based on the foundational rules 
established by ancient scriptural scholars like Manu and Yajnavalkya, which 
were designed to prevent unions that violated acceptable relational 
boundaries. Therefore, genealogists must verify that a bride is not the 
daughter of the groom's step-maternal uncle before authorizing a marriage. 
How is the 16 Chhathi calculation structured for verification? 
The calculation and verification of the16Chhathiare structured around a 
meticulous genealogical charting process known as theUtedh, which 
systematically measures the genetic distance between a prospective bride and 
groom. 
The structure of this verification is executed by the Panjikars (genealogists) 
through the following steps: 
1. Mapping the 32Mools(Ancestral Roots)To begin the verification, the 
Panjikar must chart the Utedh, which requires compiling the ancestral history 
of32 specific rootsfor both the bride and the groom. This comprehensive

record maps out six generations of the paternal lineage (Pitru-kula) and five 
generations of the maternal lineage (Matru-kula). From these combined 
lineages, the genealogist isolates 16 specific ancestors from whom the bride 
or groom is exactly the sixth descendant. 
2. Identifying the Specific 16Chhathi Before authorizing any marriage, the 
Panjikar first mathematically calculates and identifies the bride's 16 Chhathi. 
The calculation traces ancestors in a descending order, pinpointing precise 
biological intersections across both sides of her family. The structure of these 
16 ancestors is highly specific: 
• 
1st Chhathi: The bride's Vriddha Prapitamaha (her great-grandfather's 
grandfather). 
• 
2nd to 8th Chhathi: These map the maternal branches within her father's 
side, such as her great-grandfather's father-in-law (2nd Chhathi), her 
grandfather's maternal grandfather's father (3rd Chhathi), or her 
grandmother's maternal grandfather (8th Chhathi). 
• 
9th to 16th Chhathi: These map the complex branches of her mother's 
biological ancestry, starting from her maternal grandfather's great-
grandfather (9th Chhathi) and extending all the way to her maternal 
grandmother's maternal grandmother's maternal grandfather (16th 
Chhathi). 
3. Cross-Referencing with the Groom's ChartOnce all 16 of the bride's 
Chhathi are identified, the Panjikar creates the groom's Utedh. The core of 
the verification is to check if any of the bride's 16 specific Chhathi ancestors 
appear anywhere within the groom's recorded ancestry. 
4. Applying the Rules of Prohibition and ExceptionThe final marital 
eligibility is determined by where the overlapping ancestor falls in the 
groom's chart: 
• 
The Prohibition: If even one of the 16 Chhathi ancestors appears 
anywhere in the groom's paternal lineage (Pitru-paksha), the marriage is 
strictly forbidden. 
• 
The Exception: If the shared Chhathi ancestor appears exclusively within 
the groom's maternal lineage (Matru-paksha), the shared ancestry is 
considered to have adequately ceased, and the marriage is authorized. 
The system structures this verification with absolute equality;all 
16Chhathihold equal importance and authority. The ancient scriptural

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 165 
rules do not grant any special privilege or exemption to any single ancestor 
among the sixteen, ensuring the genetic boundary is never compromised. 
What criteria determines if an ancestor is in the maternal side? 
In the Panji Prabandha system, an individual's ancestry is broadly divided 
into two primary clans: the Pitru-kula (paternal clan) and the Matru-kula 
(maternal clan). 
The specific criterion that determines whether an ancestor belongs to the 
maternal or paternal side is defined by the following strict genealogical rule: 
• 
The Paternal Classification Rule: The records explicitly state that both 
the paternal and maternal lineages of a person's father and grandfather 
are legally classified as the person's paternal side (Pitru-kula). This 
means that even if an ancestor comes from the father's mother's family 
(such as the father's maternal grandfather), they are strictly counted as 
part of the paternal side, not the maternal side. 
• 
The Maternal Classification Rule: Consequently, an ancestor is only 
considered to be on the maternal side (Matru-paksha or Matru-kula) if 
they belong exclusively to the biological lineage of the individual's own 
mother. 
This defining criterion is directly reflected in how genealogists calculate the 
16 Chhathi (sixth-generation ancestors): 
• 
The 1st through 8th Chhathis map out the father's side, which includes 
the father's maternal ancestors (e.g., the father's maternal grandfather). 
• 
The 9th through 16th Chhathis strictly isolate the ancestors on the 
mother's side, tracing back exclusively through the maternal grandfather 
and maternal grandmother's lineages. 
Furthermore, ancient Dharmashastra texts explicitly define specific maternal 
relatives (Matru Bandhava) as the descendants of the mother's maternal 
uncle, the mother's father's sister, and the mother's mother's sister, reinforcing 
the strict biological boundaries of the maternal clan.

Why is Same-Gotra marriage forbidden in the system? 
In the Panji Prabandha system,Sam-Gotra(same Gotra) marriages are strictly 
forbidden because a Gotra functions as a direct indicator of patriarchal 
lineage (Vamsha-bodhak). 
Every Gotra traces its members back to a single, ancient founding sage or 
patriarch (such as Shandilya or Bharadwaj). Consequently, if a prospective 
bride and groom belong to the same Gotra, it signifies that they are 
descendants of the exact same ancient ancestor, indicating a shared blood 
relationship (Rakta Sambandha) between them. 
The primary purpose of the Panji genealogical system is to prevent 
consanguinity. Following the fundamental rules of marital eligibility 
established by ancient scholars like Manu and Yajnavalkya, a marriage is 
only permissible if the couple does not share the same Gotra or the same 
Pravara (sage lineage). 
To absolutely guarantee that a Sam-Gotra union does not take place, the 
genealogists (Panjikars) must meticulously verify the Gotra, Pravara, and 
Mool (root family) of both individuals before authorizing the marriage.

Based on the genealogical records, these categories are defined as follows:

Shrotriya (Soti): Positioned at the absolute top of the social hierarchy, 
this group is traditionally referred to as Pubaripar. Their genealogical 
records, which are sometimes called Laukik, are highly exclusive and

The Mechanics of the HierarchyA defining characteristic of this ranking 
system is that status is inherently downward-trending (Adhogami). If 
members of a high-ranking family (such as a Shrotriya) engage in marriage 
alliances with lower-ranking families, their elevated status steadily 
deteriorates and eventually ends. The genealogical texts note that due to the 
passage of time and such inter-tier marriages, the absolute first and second 
categories of this hierarchy eventually ceased to exist. 
Scriptural Equality vs. Social StratificationWhile the branch books strictly 
enforced these divisions to create "high" and "low" societal statuses, the 
records explicitly point out the irony of the system. From the strict, original 
perspective of the Dharmashastras (ancient Hindu scriptures), there is 
absolutely no difference between a Shrotriya, a Yogya, a Panjibaddha, or a 
Jaewar. The fundamental rules of consanguinity and religious duty dictate 
exactly the same system for all of them, rendering the social hierarchy a 
purely man-made construct.

classified into the rigid three-tier hierarchy: the Ayant Shreshtha (first 
category), Dwitiya Shreni (second category), and Madhyam Mul (third

• 
Status Based on Merit and Contribution: The historical records 
emphasize that the prominence or "high" and "low" status of a specific 
Mul was originally not arbitrary. Instead, a root family's distinction was 
based on their deeds (Karma), sacraments (Sanskara), and specific 
scholarly or scriptural contributions (Shastriya Yogdan) to the society, 
which collectively enriched the region of Mithila. 
• 
Comprehensive Documentation: The massive genealogical branch books 
(Shakha Pustaks) were structured to meticulously record the descendants, 
historical migrations, and detailed personal histories of all 167 Mul 
villages. This ensured that the complete genetic map of the society was 
preserved without leaving any family undocumented. 
What is the role of a Panjikar in Mithila? 
In Mithila, aPanjikarserves as an officially designated genealogist and a 
protector of the community's scriptural marital laws. Originally appointed by 
royal decree during the reign of Maharaja Harisimhadeva in 1326 AD, their 
role is central to the functioning and preservation of the vast Panji Prabandha 
genealogical system.

The primary responsibilities and roles of a Panjikar include: 
• 
Verifying Marital Eligibility: A Panjikar's most critical role is to 
mathematically verify that a prospective bride and groom do not share 
ancestors within forbidden degrees of consanguinity. To do this, they 
chart the Utedh (a genealogical map) for the couple and verify that none 
of the 16 specific Chhathi (sixth-generation ancestors) overlap 
inappropriately between the maternal and paternal lines. 
• 
Issuing the Siddhanta (Authorization): If the proposed marriage strictly 
adheres to the scriptural rules of genetic distance, the Panjikar issues a 
formal authorization certificate known as a Siddhanta or Aswajana Patra. 
Without this official written approval from the Panjikar, a marriage is 
considered unscriptural and invalid within Maithil society. 
• 
Data Collection and Record Maintenance: Historically, Panjikars showed 
immense dedication by travelling extensively from village to village, 
often enduring great hardships without royal financial protection, to 
interview family heads. They meticulously recorded new births, 
marriages, shifting lineages, and educational achievements, preserving 
this complex data in massive palm-leaf registers. 
• 
Managing Social Hierarchy: While their original 14th-century role was 
purely scientific and egalitarian, it was fundamentally altered during the 
reign of Maharaja Madhav Singh. Panjikars were ordered to compile 
Shakha Pustaks (branch books) that categorized society into rigid, 
hierarchical tiers like Shrotriya (Soti), Yogya (Jog), and Panjibaddha. 
• 
Navigating Social Pressures: As the society became rigidly stratified, the 
Panjikars' traditional integrity was severely tested. The rise of the Bikaua 
class—men who engaged in rampant polygamy to sell their high social 
status—often forced Panjikars to navigate and formally authorize absurd, 
unscriptural relational dynamics to accommodate the community's 
obsession with social rank.

male ancestors (Biji Purush) of specific family lineages resided

Tell me more about the later reforms by Maharaja Madhav Singh. 
Maharaja Madhav Singh's reforms, enacted around1760 AD, fundamentally 
shifted the Panji Prabandha from an egalitarian genealogical database to a 
rigidly stratified social system. 
According to historical records,these reforms were heavily influenced by 
certain sycophantic courtiers(described as "intellectually luxurious" 
individuals) who persuaded the King to issue a royal decree ordering the 
Panjikars to compile the *ShakhaPustaks* (branch books). 
The key changes and consequences of his reforms included: 
• 
Abandonment of the Equal Mool Panji: Before his decree, genealogists 
solely maintained the Mool Panji, which treated all registered families as 
absolute equals and was completely sufficient on its own for 
scientifically verifying marital eligibility. 
• 
Creation of Separate Tiered Records: Madhav Singh ordered the creation 
of distinct, separate branch books tailored to forcefully divide the society 
into the hierarchical categories of Soti (Shrotriya), Jog (Yogya), and 
Panjibaddha. 
• 
Emergence of a New Sub-caste: Following the compilation of these 
newly segregated books, the rigid classifications took permanent hold. 
This eventually led to the Shrotriya category crystallizing into an entirely 
new, exclusive Brahmin sub-caste in Mithila around 1800 AD. 
• 
Institutionalisation of Social Evils: By arbitrarily imposing "high" and 
"low" ranks based on these records, the reforms replaced a functional,

Are there other recorded instances of caste-status upgrades in the light of 
Dooshan Panji? 
In the light of the provided sources, theDooshanPanji(often referred to as 
"The Black Book") was actually not used to record financial 
transactions for status upgrades. Instead, it was a highly secretive 
genealogical register dedicated exclusively to documentingflaws, 
degradations, or "impurities" (*Doshas)* that had entered a family's lineage. 
The Dooshan Panji was maintained by genealogists to secretly track societal 
and genetic realities that deviated from strict scriptural norms. Its recorded 
instances include: 
• 
Inter-caste Marriages: It explicitly documented marriages between 
Maithil Brahmins and people of other castes, specifically noting unions 
with Dalits (such as Charmakarinis, or tanners) and Muslims. 
• 
Widow Remarriages and Unconventional Births: The book kept written 
documentary evidence of women remarrying after their husband's death, 
as well as children born outside of conventional timelines. 
A prominent historical example of what the Dooshan Panji actually recorded 
is the case of the great Navya-Nyaya philosopher,Gangesha Upadhyaya. 
The secret records explicitly note the "flaw" that he was born five years after 
his father's death, and that he himself later married a Charmakarini. 
Therefore, while cash-based status upgrades were facilitated through official 
"genealogy-based upgradation orders" (like those issued by the Darbhanga 
Raj), the Dooshan Panji served an entirely different purpose: acting as a 
hidden database of unscriptural marriages and genetic "flaws" so that 
genealogists could prevent those specific lineages from unknowingly causing 
negative consequences in future marital alliances. 
Explain the relation between Gangesha Upadhyaya and Dooshan Panji. 
The relationship between the great Navya-Nyaya philosopher Gangesha 
Upadhyaya and the Dooshan Panji (The Black Book) centers around the fact 
that this highly secretive genealogical register explicitly recorded societal 
and genetic "flaws" (Doshas) regarding his birth and marriage. 
As the founder of the New Logic school of philosophy and author of the 
famous text Tattvachintamani, Gangesha Upadhyaya's legacy is deeply tied 
to the Dooshan Panji, which documented two major deviations from strict 
scriptural norms in his life:

• 
Unconventional Birth: The Dooshan Panji contains an ancient scribal 
note stating that Gangesha was born five years after the death of his 
father (recorded in the texts as "Pitri parokshe panch varsh vyatite 
Gangeshotpattiḥ"). 
• 
Inter-caste Marriage: The secret book explicitly records that Gangesha 
married a Charmakarini (a woman from the tanner community, which 
was considered a Dalit caste). Different entries within the records refer to 
his wife as Vallabha, Medha, or Aananda. 
The Historical Cover-up and "Honour Killing"The presence of these 
entries in the Dooshan Panji led to a massive historical cover-up. According 
to the sources, later 20th-century scholars like Prof. Ramanath Jha were 
ashamed of Gangesha's roots and offspring due to these unscriptural realities. 
As a result, they chose to keep the Dooshan Panji a closely guarded secret. 
This suppression is described in the texts as the "honour killing" of Gangesha 
Upadhyaya's legacy. Because the records were hidden, these scholars falsely 
informed researchers (such as Dinesh Chandra Bhattacharya in 1958) that 
Gangesha's family was completely ignored, his father's name was unknown, 
and his lineage had quickly dwindled into insignificance and gone extinct. It 
was not until the Dooshan Panji was finally released in 2009 that these 
hidden truths about his life were brought to light. 
Additionally, the explicit documentation of his marriage to the Charmakarini 
Aananda found within the Dooshan Panji later served as the factual 
inspiration for the Maithili love story *'Shabdashastram'* ('The Science of 
Words'), authored by Gajendra Thakur. 
How did Maharaja Rameshwar Singh's orders affect the social hierarchy? 
Maharaja Rameshwar Singh's orders fundamentally impacted the social 
hierarchy by formalizing the stratification of thePachhbaripar(the general 
category for the remaining Brahmins who were not classified in the top 
Shrotriya tier) and institutionalising the practice of buying social rank. 
Specifically, his directives affected the hierarchy in the following ways: 
• 
Creation of a 15-Tier System: Under the Maharaja's orders, the 
genealogists (Panjikars) formalized the genealogical records and social 
standing for the Pachhbaripar, dividing these families into 15 distinct 
hierarchical categories. 
• 
Monetization of Social Status: This formal categorization process 
sparked massive disputes and controversies throughout the community.

The records explicitly note that there was widespread outrage because 
official Panji recognition and elevated status within these new tiers were 
granted to so-called "lower" families purely in exchange for money. 
• 
Direct Purchases of Lineage: Wealthy individuals could now officially 
buy their way into the hierarchy. A notable example is Lakshmipati 
Mishra, a wealthy man known as a "Navalakhadhipati", who secured his 
official Panji classification (in the Shandilya Gotra) by giving a 
"donation" of 1. lakh Takas directly to Maharaja Rameshwar Singh. 
By legally establishing these 15 tiers while simultaneously allowing ranks to 
be purchased for cash, his orders entrenched financial corruption deeply into 
the region's genealogical and social systems. 
How did the 15 categories for Pachhbaripar Brahmins create conflict? 
The creation of the 15 hierarchical categories for thePachhbaripar(also 
recorded as Chhabaripar) Brahmins generated significant conflict because it 
exposed the blatant monetization of the genealogical system. 
When the genealogists (Panjikars), acting on the orders of the 
Maharajadhiraj, established and initially published this 1 to 15 tier system, 
they invited the community to submit any written objections or feedback. 
The plan was to have a special assembly review these concerns before 
making the final publication. 
However, massive disputes (vivad) immediately erupted across the 
community. The core of the outrage was the widespread realization that 
official Panji recognition and placement within these newly formed tiers 
were being granted to numerous so-called "lower" families purely in 
exchange for money (pai lae). This confirmed to the community that the 
hierarchy was compromised, as wealthy individuals were effectively buying 
their way into formalized social ranks. 
What does the Shrotriya tier's exclusion of earlier categories mean? 
The Shrotriya tier's exclusion and replacement of earlier categories signifies 
thecomplete transformation of theMaithilgenealogical system from a 
merit-based record into a rigid, sub-caste social hierarchy. 
The significance of this shift is defined by the following points: 
• 
Erasure of Merit-Based Status: Originally, the system identified 34 or 35 
prominent root families (Mools) based on their actual deeds (Karma), 
sacraments, and scriptural contributions, classifying them into the Ayant

• 
Creation of an Artificial Sub-Caste: When Maharaja Madhav Singh 
ordered the creation of the separate Shakha Pustaks (branch books), this 
original structure was abandoned in favour of the artificially created 
Shrotriya (Soti), Yogya (Jog), and Panjibaddha tiers. Consequently, the 
Shrotriya category eventually crystallized into an entirely new and

•

Shrotriya-led hierarchy was that status was strictly downward-trending 
(Adhogami). If members of a high-ranking family married into lower

explicitly note that because of these strict marriage rules and the 
resulting social degradation, the original first and second categories 
(Ayant Shreshtha and Dwitiya Shreni) completely ceased to exist. 
Which royal families issued these cash-based upgradation orders? 
The cash-based genealogy upgradation orders were issued by theDarbhanga 
Raj(the Maharajas of Darbhanga). 
The historical records and annexures containing the documentary proof of 
these transactions are specifically linked to the Darbhanga royal family, such 
as Maharaja Rameshwar Singh, who received the 1. lakh Takas payment for 
a status upgrade. 
The sources provide an interesting historical context regarding this royal 
family's influence: 
• 
British Indian Zamindars: The texts note that the Maharajas of 
Darbhanga were actually "permanent settlement zamindars of 
Cornwallis" operating within British India. It was under their authority 
that these official genealogy-based upgradation orders were issued to 
wealthy individuals. 
• 
The Contrast with Nepal: The records highlight that because there were 
no such zamindars or royal families issuing these types of status orders in 
the Nepalese part of Mithila, the cash-purchased sub-castes never took 
root there. Consequently, while the Darbhanga Raj's orders created the 
exclusive "Shrotriya" sub-caste in British India around 1800 AD, no such 
sub-caste exists among the Maithil Brahmins in Nepal today.

What are the specific marital rules for different hierarchical tiers? 
The specific marital rules for the different hierarchical tiers in the Panji 
Prabandha system primarily revolved around the required generational 
distance (Sapinda) and the strict downward-trending nature of social status. 
According to the genealogical records, the specific rules were enforced as 
follows: 
• 
Generational Distance for Shrotriyas: The highest-ranking Shrotriyas 
were required to exclude 5 generations on the mother's side (Matri-
paksha) and 7 generations on the father's side (Pitri-paksha) when 
calculating marital eligibility. 
• 
Generational Distance for Panjibaddhas: Families in the Panjibaddha tier 
(often referred to as Vanshaj) were required to exclude 5 generations on 
the mother's side, but only 6 generations on the father's side. 
• 
The Rule of Degradation (Adhogami): A fundamental rule of this 
hierarchy was that social status and lineage purity ("Panji and Pani") 
were strictly downward-trending. If a higher-ranking family married into 
a lower-ranking family (Nimna Kul), their elevated status systematically 
deteriorated over time and eventually ceased to exist. 
The Scriptural ContradictionDespite the strict enforcement of these 
differing generational rules to separate the tiers, the texts point out a major 
contradiction. According to the original Dharmashastras (ancient Hindu 
scriptures), there is actually a single, uniform arrangement for everyone. 
Whether someone is a Shrotriya, a Yogya, a Panjibaddha, or an unrecorded 
Jaewar, the fundamental scriptural rules for marriage are completely 
identical, noting that the different marital rules applied to the tiers were an 
arbitrary societal construct. 
Are marital rules the same for the Jaewar category? 
Yes, according to the ancient scriptures (Dharmashastras), the marital rules 
are exactly the same for theJaewarcategory. 
The Jaewar were individuals and families who were left out of the formal, 
tiered classifications and were not officially registered as Panjibaddha. 
However, the genealogical texts explicitly state that from a strict scriptural 
perspective, there is a single, uniform arrangement applied to everyone. 
The rules of consanguinity and marital eligibility are completely identical 
whether someone is a high-ranking Shrotriya, a Yogya, a Panjibaddha, or an

unrecorded Jaewar, meaning the differing rules later enforced by the tiered 
hierarchy were an arbitrary societal construct. 
What are the common generational rules for all tiers? 
According to the ancient Dharmashastras, the fundamental marital and 
generational rules are entirely identical for all Maithil Brahmins, regardless 
of whether they belong to the Shrotriya, Yogya, Panjibaddha, or Jaewar tiers. 
The genealogical texts outline seven uniform rules that apply to everyone for 
verifying marital eligibility: 
• 
Same Caste (Swajati): The prospective bride and groom must belong to 
the same caste. 
• 
Different Lineage: The couple must not share the same Gotra (sage 
lineage) or Pravara (sage sub-lineage). 
• 
Generational Distance (Sapinda): To prevent consanguinity, the bride 
must not be a descendant within five generations on the groom's mother's 
side (Matri-paksha) and within six generations (traditionally cited as 
seven) on the groom's father's side (Pitri-paksha). 
• 
The Step-Mother Rule (Kathmama): The bride cannot be a descendant of 
the groom's stepmother's brother. 
• 
Grandparent Exclusions: The bride must not be the direct offspring of the 
groom's paternal or maternal grandfather. 
• 
Sibling Hierarchy: A younger sibling is not permitted to marry if their 
older sibling remains unmarried. 
• 
Living Wife's Sister: A man is forbidden from marrying the biological 
sister of his currently living wife. 
The records emphasize that these specific directives form a single, 
scientifically and scripturally sound framework designed to ensure genetic 
distance and the birth of healthy offspring, proving that the differing rules 
later enforced to separate the social tiers were purely artificial constructs. 
What is the 'Rule of 16' for sixth-generation ancestors? 
The"Rule of 16"refers to the 16 specific ancestral figures—known as 
theChhathi—from whom a prospective bride or groom is exactly the sixth-
generation descendant. 
In the Panji Prabandha system, these 16 ancestors are mathematically 
isolated by mapping out the 32 root families (Mools) across both the paternal 
and maternal lineages.

Identifying the 16Chhathi The genealogist (Panjikar) identifies these 16 
distinct generational nodes for both the bride and the groom. The list covers 
complex relational branches, such as: 
• 
1st Chhathi: The grandfather of the great-grandfather. 
• 
8th Chhathi: The maternal grandfather of the paternal grandmother. 
• 
16th Chhathi: The maternal grandfather of the maternal grandmother. 
The Marital Test (Consanguinity Rules)All 16 Chhathi hold equal 
importance in the verification process. Once the 16 sixth-generation 
ancestors are identified for the bride, they are cross-referenced with the 
groom's chart using strict scriptural logic: 
• 
The Paternal Block: If even a single one of the bride's 16 Chhathi 
ancestors appears anywhere in the groom's paternal lineage (Pitri-
paksha), the marriage is strictly forbidden and deemed invalid. This is 
because scriptural rules require a genetic distance of more than six 
generations on the father's side. 
• 
The Maternal Allowance: However, if that exact same sixth-generation 
ancestor appears in the groom's maternal lineage (Matri-paksha), the 
marriage is officially authorized. This is because the consanguinity rule 
for the mother's side only requires an exclusion of five generations; 
therefore, a sixth-generation connection is perfectly acceptable. 
This rule applies equally to both the bride and the groom, ensuring that the 
genetic distance between the two families strictly adheres to the ancient 
Dharmashastras. 
Are the marital rules for women also documented? 
Yes, the marital rules and genealogical details are meticulously documented 
for women, and the fundamental rules of consanguinity apply equally to both 
the bride (Kanya) and the groom (Var). 
The records highlight several specific details regarding how women are 
treated within the Panji Prabandha system: 
• 
Equal Application of Consanguinity Rules: The ancient scriptures 
explicitly dictate that just as a groom must avoid specific generational 
overlaps, a bride is only considered eligible and highly recommended for 
marriage if she is not a Sapinda (a blood relative within forbidden 
degrees) of either her mother's or her father's side.

systematically map out her direct descending lineage—from her great-
grandparents down to her parents—to mathematically isolate her exact 
16 Chhathi (sixth-generation ancestors) across both her maternal and 
paternal lines. This chart is then cross-referenced with the groom's chart 
to ensure there is no genetic overlap.

documented and preserved the names of women unbroken, generation

By recording the female lineages with the exact same mathematical precision 
as the male lineages, the genealogists ensured that the genetic logic of the

How did Panjikars document women's names historically? 
Historically, Panjikars took a remarkably progressive approach to 
documenting women's names, especially considering the era. In a highly 
male-dominated society where women's identities and names were often 
forgotten or excluded from historical texts, the Panji Prabandha system 
deliberatelypreservedwomen's names continuously from generation to 
generation. 
The specific ways they documented women included:

Recording Unbroken Lineages: To map out the complex genetic 
networks, Panjikars meticulously recorded the births and marriages of 
women just as they did for men. If a family had a daughter instead of a 
son, the genealogist would explicitly record her in the registers using the 
terms Suta (daughter) or Sutascha (and daughters) to ensure the lineage 
was not lost. 
• 
Formatting the Bride's Details: When verifying a marriage, the 
genealogist systematically charted the bride's ancestry. In the Utedh

residing village and her ancient root village (Mool Gram) on the left side

Could you provide information on the judgment concerning Mahesh Thakur 
in respect of Panaji? 
Mahamahopadhyaya Maharaja Mahesh Thakur made a significant ruling 
(known as a Vyavastha) regarding the rules of consanguinity and 
generational distance (Sapinda) used to determine marital eligibility in the 
Panji system. 
According to the ancient scriptural directives, a prospective bride and groom 
must exclude five generations on the mother's side (Matri-paksha) and seven 
generations on the father's side (Pitri-paksha). This traditional rule was stated 
as *"Matritahpanchamimtyaktvapitritahsaptamimtyajet"* (abandon the 
fifth on the mother's side and the seventh on the father's side). 
However,Mahesh Thakur modified this traditional interpretation 
to"*SaptamimBhajet", specifically ruling that the seventh generation on the 
father's side is actually acceptable (graahya) and authorized for marriage. 
Under this arrangement, the father's lineage is counted up to seven 
generations, where one ancestor is the giver of the Pinda, three are sharers of 
the Pinda (Pinda Bhagi), and three partake in the remnants (Lep Bhagi*). 
This specific ruling provided by Mahesh Thakur was happily accepted 
by the society and became the officially recognized standardfor verifying 
marital eligibility within the Panji records.

What is the significance of the Dooshan Panji? 
TheDooshanPanji (often referred to as the "impure secret book" or "The 
Black Book") is one of the seven types of genealogical registers (Panji) 
maintained in the Maithil Brahmin tradition. Its primary significance lies in 
its highly secretive and objective documentation of societal and genetic 
"flaws" (Doshas) or instances of degradation (Ksharan) that entered various 
family lineages. 
The core significance of the Dooshan Panji can be understood through the 
following key aspects: 
• 
Recording Societal Deviations: It served as the official repository for 
documenting unscriptural realities and "burning questions" of the time. 
This included explicit records of inter-caste marriages (such as unions 
with individuals from the Charmakar or Muslim communities), 
marriages with unknown maternal lineages, and highly unconventional 
births. For example, as discussed earlier, it is the text that recorded the 
Navya-Nyaya philosopher Gangesha Upadhyaya being born five years 
after his father's death and marrying a Charmakarini. 
• 
Tracking Lineage Purification: The purpose of documenting these 
genetic and societal flaws was not to permanently condemn a family, but 
to track the lineage so that it could be saved from future negative 
consequences regarding marital eligibility. By mathematically mapping 
these "impure" branches, the system could identify when a bloodline was 
officially "purified" over time. The text provides an example of a lineage 
stemming from an inter-caste marriage that was mathematically deemed 
"completely pure" (Purnatah Shuddha) by the time it reached the sixth 
generation. 
• 
Objective Historical Transparency: The Dooshan Panji demonstrates that 
ancient genealogists recorded historical events and offspring without 
prejudice or permanent malice. It highlights an advanced historical 
consciousness where the society objectively documented reality,

intentionally suppressed by later 20th-century scholars (like Prof. 
Ramanath Jha) who were ashamed of these unscriptural realities. This

cover-up of certain legacies until the book was finally released to the 
public in 2009.
